content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Non-structural compensatory reform (leave selection age unchanged)'. Demographic decline through 2040 will force school-network consolidation and closure of small selective tracks independent of policy choice, but government's simultaneous use of score-threshold levers is accelerating selective-track contraction beyond what demographic decline alone would cause, concentrating remaining grammar-school slots among already-advantaged urban populations.
opportunities: Achievement gains in the range of Portugal's observed 0.3 SD improvement over roughly 15-18 years are plausible for the comprehensive (non-tracked) majority of Hungarian pupils if curriculum, autonomy, and assessment reforms are implemented with comparable fidelity.

Politically less disruptive than structural reform since it does not touch the statutory definition of secondary education, avoiding the felmenő-rendszer obligations and the direct Fidesz 'totalitarian unified model' framing risk that S3 faces — though this should not be read as evidence the package is politically risk-free, given that targeted funding for disadvantaged schools is independently a fiscal-consolidation target.
strengths: Annual births in Hungary fell from approximately 125,000 in the early 1990s to 77,500 in 2024, with a further 7.1% decline to ~72,000 in 2025, representing the lowest recorded level since 1949 and forcing school-network consolidation decisions in the 2020s–2030s regardless of selection policy.

Primary general school (általános iskola) enrollment declined 17.3% over 20 years, from 859,000 pupils in 2005/2006 to 710,000 in 2024/2025, tracking the shrinking birth cohorts directly, with first-grade intake falling 1.9% year-on-year in 2024/2025 to 95,000.
threats: Targeted TEIP-style funding lines are likely first-line cuts during fiscal consolidation (deficit projected at 6.2% in 2026, debt rising to 76.8% of GDP by 2027), making the central benefit-delivery mechanism budget-vulnerable in a way that significantly undermines the durability of any expected equity gain, even though the scenario avoids the structural-reform reversal risks facing S1-S3.

The scenario's political attractiveness as a 'lower-risk' alternative may itself become a political liability if framed by reform advocates or the European Commission as inadequate given Hungary's documented status as the EU's most socially selective system, risking accusations of policy avoidance rather than genuine reform.
weaknesses: The net causal effect of early selection (6/8-year grammar-school entry at age 10–12) on individual student achievement and social mobility is not directly measured in Hungarian evidence; test-score gaps at selection reflect composition, not school value-added.

The relationship between consolidation-induced school closure, teacher workforce redeployment, and pedagogical quality in surviving small schools is unknown; closing small grammar schools may displace experienced teachers or may improve efficiency.
